result:
I keep a folder on my laptop called “Seeds.” It’s where I paste half-formed paragraphs that arrive while I’m supposed to be doing something else—grocery lists, quarterly reports, pretending to listen at dinner. Last night I opened the folder for the first time in months and found a file named simply “Tuesday,” time-stamped 3:14 a.m. on a Tuesday I no longer remember being awake for. It contains one sentence:

The moon tonight is the color of old piano keys, and something in its light keeps resetting the clocks.

I have no idea what I meant, only that reading it felt like discovering a bruise whose origin you can’t recall. So I walked outside in search of the moon at 3:14 a.m. again, just to check if any of the clocks had shifted. They hadn’t; but the neighbor’s cat—a black, incurious creature named Schrodinger by children who have never spelled the word—was sitting atop my car as though moonlight were soldered him to the roof. I sat on the driveway beside him and we shared the silence typically reserved for funerals or first kisses. After a while he began to purr, low and mechanical, like a distant car engine trying to remember how to be warm. I thought: maybe the sentence isn’t a metaphor but a recording. One of the clocks in my kitchen did lose three minutes last week; maybe that was the reset. Maybe the moon is a bored technician.

When I came back inside, another new file had appeared in Seeds: a blank document titled “Wednesday.” It was 3:17 a.m. The cursor blinked like a lighthouse that couldn’t decide who needed saving.

The moon tonight is the color of old piano keys, and something in its light keeps resetting the clocks.

I have no idea what I meant, only that reading it felt like discovering a bruise whose origin you can’t recall. So I walked outside in search of the moon at 3:14 a.m. again, just to check if any of the clocks had shifted. They hadn’t; but the neighbor’s cat—a black, incurious creature named Schrodinger by children who have never spelled the word—was sitting atop my car as though moonlight were soldered him to the roof. I sat on the driveway beside him and we shared the silence typically reserved for funerals or first kisses. After a while he began to purr, low and mechanical, like a distant car engine trying to remember how to be warm. I thought: maybe the sentence isn’t a metaphor but a recording. One of the clocks in my kitchen did lose three minutes last week; maybe that was the reset. Maybe the moon is a bored technician.

When I came back inside, another new file had appeared in Seeds: a blank document titled “Wednesday.” It was 3:17 a.m. The cursor blinked like a lighthouse that couldn’t decide who needed saving.
